response: The internet's history began in the 1960s with U.S. Department of Defense-funded ARPANET, which was demonstrated in 1969, and grew through the 1970s, connecting universities and research institutions; the 1983 transition to TCP/IP protocols laid the foundation for the modern internet, followed by Tim Berners-Lee's 1989 invention of the World Wide Web (with HTML, URLs, HTTP) and 1993's Mosaic browser, which made the web accessible to the public. The late 1990s dot-com boom saw massive investment in internet companies, many of which collapsed in the 2001 crash, though survivors like Amazon and Google became highly valuable; the mid-2000s brought social media (Facebook, Twitter, YouTube) transforming communication, while the 2007 launch of the iPhone powered mobile internet, making the web ubiquitous, and 2006's AWS kickstarted cloud computing. Today, the internet connects over 5 billion people and underpins nearly all aspects of modern life, from commerce to communication to entertainment.
